title: Adaptive point count scaling
summary: Widen the point-count gap between device tiers so desktop renders significantly more points than mobile, while guaranteeing every tier stays volumetric 3D. Most infrastructure already exists (quality tiers, density mapping, per-system maxPoints). The work is primarily tuning constants, adding a dedicated scaling helper, and writing targeted tests.

steps.title: Widen maxPoints spread across quality tiers
steps.description: Increase the ratio between low and high tier maxPoints so desktop is at least 5× mobile. Current values (200/500/1200) give a 6× ratio but effective counts are compressed by the density×complexity multiplier. Raise high-tier maxPoints to ~2000 and keep low-tier at 200 to guarantee a significant gap after the density formula is applied.
changes: Update HIGH tier maxPoints from 1200 to 2000
Update HIGH tier maxRibbonPoints from 1000 to 1600
Update HIGH tier maxParticles from 600 to 1000
Keep LOW tier values unchanged (200/200/150) to preserve mobile budget
Update MEDIUM tier maxPoints from 500 to 800, maxRibbonPoints from 500 to 700, maxParticles from 350 to 550
steps.rationale: After density (0.3–1.0) and complexity (0.2–1.0) multipliers, the effective low-tier count lands around 48–120 points and high-tier around 960–2000. This ensures desktop renders 8–16× more points than low-end mobile.

steps.title: Extract shared adaptive count helper
steps.description: The effective point count formula (density × maxPoints × complexityFactor) is duplicated across all four geometry systems. Extract it into a shared exported function so the scaling logic lives in one place.
changes: Create and export function computeAdaptiveCount(density: number, structureComplexity: number, maxPoints: number): number that encapsulates the floor(density * maxPoints * (0.6 + complexity * 0.4)) formula with clamping
Add a minimum floor of 24 points (enough for visible volumetric shape) instead of current minimum of 1
Import and use this helper in pointCloud.ts, particleField.ts, ribbonField.ts, and crystalField.ts replacing their inline calculations
steps.rationale: Single source of truth for the scaling formula. The 24-point minimum ensures even the lowest tier renders a recognisable 3D form, not a single dot.

steps.title: Refactor all geometry systems to use shared helper
steps.description: Replace each system's inline point count calculation with a call to the shared computeAdaptiveCount helper.
changes: Import computeAdaptiveCount in each system file
Replace inline effectiveCount calculation with helper call
Remove any per-system minimum clamping that conflicts with the shared minimum
steps.rationale: Consistency: all geometry systems scale identically relative to their tier's maxPoints budget.

steps.title: Verify density mapping produces adequate spread
steps.description: Check that capabilityToDensity produces values that, when combined with the new maxPoints, yield the required gap. Current formula (dpr×0.4 + cores×0.6, range 0.3–1.0) already biases heavily toward core count. No change expected unless testing reveals the spread is still too narrow.
changes: Review capabilityToDensity function — no code change expected
If mobile density lands above 0.5 for typical phones (DPR=2, cores=4), lower the floor or adjust weights to push mobile density toward 0.3–0.4
steps.rationale: The density multiplier is the second lever (after maxPoints) that determines the effective gap. It must not compress the tiers back together.

steps.title: Write acceptance tests
steps.description: Add targeted tests for all four acceptance criteria using the same vitest+jsdom setup as existing tests.

tests.name: Desktop uses ≥5× more points than mobile
tests.approach: Call computeQuality with high-end desktop signals (DPR=2, cores=16, memory=32, no touch) and low-end mobile signals (DPR=2, cores=4, memory=4, touch). Compute effective point counts via computeAdaptiveCount for each. Assert desktop count / mobile count ≥ 5.

tests.name: Mobile maintains volumetric depth (≥24 points, 3D positions)
tests.approach: Create a pointCloud with low-tier QualityProfile and minimum density (0.3). Assert effectiveCount ≥ 24. Assert all generated positions have non-zero Z spread (max(z) - min(z) > 0.5).

tests.name: Scaling affects count, not dimensionality
tests.approach: For each tier (low/medium/high), create a pointCloud and verify: (a) point count increases with tier, (b) all positions are 3-component vectors, (c) Z variance is positive in every tier.

tests.name: No 2D fallback on low tier
tests.approach: Create all four geometry systems (pointCloud, particleField, ribbonField, crystalField) with low-tier profile. Assert each produces a THREE.Points mesh (not Mesh/Line), uses ShaderMaterial, and has a perspective camera (not orthographic).

tests.name: computeAdaptiveCount consistency across systems
tests.approach: For identical inputs (density, complexity, maxPoints), assert all systems produce the same effective count via the shared helper.

tests.name: Minimum point floor is enforced
tests.approach: Call computeAdaptiveCount with density=0.01, complexity=0.2, maxPoints=200. Assert result ≥ 24.

steps.title: Build verification and manual spot-check
steps.description: Run npm run build, ensure no TypeScript errors. Run vitest to confirm all new and existing tests pass. Manually verify in browser that low/high tier produce visually different densities while both remaining clearly 3D.

verification.build: npm run build must pass with zero errors
verification.tests: npx vitest run tests/visual/adaptive-point-count.test.ts must pass all 6 tests
verification.existing_tests: npx vitest run must not regress any existing tests
verification.manual: Browser console must show no WebGL errors; low tier must render a recognisable volumetric shape

risks.risk: Raising high-tier maxPoints to 2000 could hurt frame rate on mid-range desktops
risks.mitigation: The density×complexity multiplier means effective count rarely hits the cap. Monitor via debug instrumentation (FPS callback in renderLoop). Can lower to 1600 if needed.

risks.risk: 24-point minimum may look too sparse on large screens
risks.mitigation: 24 is the floor for worst-case inputs (density near 0). Typical low-tier mobile with density ~0.35 gets ~48–84 points, which is adequate for volumetric perception.

estimated_scope: small — primarily constant tuning, one helper extraction, and test authoring
